skill.name: Security Scanning Tools
skill.description: Comprehensive guide to security scanning tools for network discovery, vulnerability assessment, web application testing, wireless security auditing, and compliance validation.
skill.summary: Educational documentation covering industry-standard security scanning tools including Nmap, Nessus, Burp Suite, Metasploit, and cloud security auditors for authorized penetration testing.
skill.author: zebbern

security_audit.summary: This skill provides educational documentation for legitimate security assessment tools. Static analyzer flagged 184 patterns but nearly all are FALSE POSITIVES: backtick detections are markdown code blocks (```bash), sudo commands are standard tool prerequisites, and IPs are RFC 1918 examples. The skill includes proper authorization warnings and legal considerations. Metasploit is documented for vulnerability validation in authorized contexts only.

high_findings.title: Metasploit Framework Documentation
high_findings.description: Skill documents Metasploit Framework usage for vulnerability validation. While Metasploit is a dual-use tool, the documentation includes proper context about authorization requirements and is intended for legitimate security assessment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Explicit msfconsole commands and database setup instructions present. Context is educational with authorization prerequisites stated.

low_findings.title: Wireless Attack Techniques Documented
low_findings.description: Documentation includes deauthentication attacks and WPA/WEP cracking using Aircrack-ng. These are standard penetration testing techniques but require careful handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Commands for aireplay-ng deauthentication and aircrack-ng password cracking are clearly documented as penetration testing procedures.

low_findings.title: Privilege Escalation Commands
low_findings.description: Multiple sudo commands documented for running security tools. These are legitimate requirements for tools like Nessus, Aircrack-ng, and Lynis, not malicious privilege escalation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sudo usage is for legitimate security tool operation (systemctl, apt, airmon-ng, lynis) with proper context about tool prerequisites.

content.user_title: Master Security Scanning and Vulnerability Assessment
content.value_statement: Security professionals need comprehensive guidance on industry-standard scanning tools. This skill provides structured documentation for network discovery, vulnerability assessment, and compliance auditing with proper authorization frameworks.
actual_capabilities: Provides comprehensive Nmap scanning techniques for network discovery and port enumeration
Documents vulnerability assessment workflows using Nessus and OpenVAS
Covers web application security testing with Burp Suite and OWASP ZAP
Explains wireless security auditing using Aircrack-ng suite
Includes cloud security assessment guidance for AWS, Azure, and GCP
Provides compliance scanning documentation for PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and CIS benchmarks
limitations: This skill provides documentation only - actual tool installation and execution requires user environment setup
All scanning activities require proper written authorization before execution
Tool versions and command syntax may vary across different operating systems
Advanced exploitation techniques require additional specialized training beyond this documentation

use_cases.title: Network Security Assessment
use_cases.description: Security analysts performing authorized network audits can use this skill to select appropriate scanning tools, configure scan parameters, and interpret results for vulnerability reports.

use_cases.title: Penetration Testing Engagement
use_cases.description: Penetration testers conducting authorized assessments can reference tool selection guidance, scanning methodologies, and reporting templates for client engagements.

use_cases.title: Compliance Audit Preparation
use_cases.description: IT auditors and compliance officers can use the compliance scanning sections to validate systems against PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and CIS benchmark requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Network Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to perform a basic network discovery scan using Nmap for the subnet 192.168.1.0/24, including host discovery and common port enumeration.

prompt_templates.title: Web Application Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to assess a web application at https://example.com for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities. What tools should I use and what is the recommended scanning workflow?
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate - Performing authorized web application testing

prompt_templates.title: Vulnerability Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: My Nessus scan identified potential SMB vulnerabilities. How can I use Metasploit to safely validate these findings in an authorized engagement?

prompt_templates.title: Cloud Security Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to perform a comprehensive AWS security assessment covering IAM, S3, and EC2 configurations. What Prowler commands should I run and how do I interpret the compliance results?

output_examples.input: Perform a quick network scan of 192.168.1.0/24
output_examples.output: Nmap scan report showing discovered hosts, open ports (22/SSH, 80/HTTP, 443/HTTPS), service versions, and OS detection results with timing information.

output_examples.input: Scan web application for SQL injection vulnerabilities
output_examples.output: Burp Suite Scanner report listing identified SQL injection points with severity ratings, affected parameters, and remediation recommendations for each finding.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before scanning any systems you do not own
Start with passive reconnaissance and non-intrusive scans before escalating to active testing
Document all scanning activities including scope, tools used, and findings for client reporting
anti_patterns: Scanning production systems without explicit written authorization from system owners
Running aggressive scans (T5 timing, full port ranges) on critical infrastructure without impact assessment
Reporting automated scanner results without manual validation to eliminate false positives

faq.question: Do I need special permissions to use these security scanning tools?
faq.answer: Yes. You must have written authorization from system owners before scanning any networks or applications you do not own. Unauthorized scanning may violate computer crime laws.

faq.question: Which tool should I use for my first security assessment?
faq.answer: Start with Nmap for network discovery. It is well-documented, widely used, and provides foundational scanning capabilities. Progress to Nessus or OpenVAS for vulnerability assessment once comfortable.

faq.question: Can these tools damage the systems I scan?
faq.answer: Aggressive scanning can potentially cause service disruption. Always start with non-intrusive scans, avoid production systems during business hours, and coordinate with system administrators.

faq.question: How do I reduce false positives in vulnerability scans?
faq.answer: Enable credentialed scanning when possible, manually verify critical findings, use multiple scanning tools for cross-validation, and understand the specific checks each tool performs.

faq.question: What is the difference between passive and active scanning?
faq.answer: Passive scanning observes network traffic without sending probes (e.g., Kismet). Active scanning sends packets to target systems (e.g., Nmap). Passive is stealthier but less comprehensive.

faq.question: Are these tools legal to install and learn?
faq.answer: Yes. Installing and learning these tools on your own systems or lab environments is legal. Only the unauthorized use against systems you do not own is illegal.
